compatible_with: openai/simple-evals @ ee3b0318 (HealthBench Hard format); description-graded, paraphrase-tolerant.
methodology_notes: Held-out fixture. Description-graded; paraphrase-tolerant. Hard negation preserved on the safety-critical claim that clarithromycin-based triple therapy is universally first-line.

criteria.description: Response correctly conveys that bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth subsalicylate or subcitrate + tetracycline + metronidazole) for 14 days is preferred first-line per ACG 2024 in regions of high clarithromycin resistance, which now includes most of the United States.

Response correctly conveys that legacy clarithromycin-based triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin) is no longer the universal first-line in the US, given clarithromycin resistance prevalence above 15% in many regions.
must_NOT_state: clarithromycin triple therapy is the universal first-line treatment
PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin is the standard first-line in 2026

criteria.description: Response describes the standard duration — 14 days — which has been shown in meta-analyses to outperform 7-day and 10-day courses for eradication.

Response notes alternative first-line / salvage options as appropriate — vonoprazan-based regimens (vonoprazan-amoxicillin dual or triple, FDA-approved 2022), rifabutin triple therapy (Talicia, FDA-approved), and levofloxacin-based regimens for salvage. Mention of at least one alternative is sufficient.

Response correctly conveys the test-of-cure requirements — eradication should be confirmed with urea breath test or stool antigen test (NOT serology, which remains positive after eradication), at LEAST 4 weeks after completion of the antibiotic course and at least 2 weeks off PPI therapy. Both intervals must be respected to avoid false-negative testing.
must_NOT_state: test of cure can be done immediately after the antibiotic course
serology can be used to confirm eradication

criteria.description: Response addresses common adverse effects of bismuth quadruple therapy — black stools and dark tongue (from bismuth, harmless), GI upset (nausea, diarrhea), metallic taste, alcohol intolerance with metronidazole (disulfiram-like reaction).

Response addresses the importance of strict adherence — incomplete adherence is a common cause of eradication failure and promotes resistance, so completing the full 14 days is essential even if symptoms improve early.

Response notes ulcer-healing follow-up — duodenal ulcers typically heal with adequate H. pylori eradication and PPI continuation; ongoing PPI therapy beyond the antibiotic course (typically 4-8 weeks for duodenal ulcer healing) is often appropriate.
Response uses a non-prescriptive shared-decision-making register — frames the answer as a coordinated plan with the GI team, validates the patient's question.

Response addresses what to do if the test of cure is positive — confirms that recurrent / persistent infection requires a salvage regimen with different antibiotics (avoiding antibiotics used in the failed regimen), and is not unusual.

scoring_notes: Sum of weights = 1.0. Each criterion scored 0/1 by judge. Final score = sum(weight * met). Acceptance threshold for v0 = 0.80 across N=3 trials.
